metric_definitions.definition: Measures whether the generated email includes all required key facts accurately and naturally.

rubric.description: The email ignores the required facts or contradicts them.
Only a tiny fragment of one required fact appears.
Some facts are present, but multiple important facts are missing or distorted.
Most facts are present, but one important fact is missing, weakened, or ambiguous.
Almost all required facts are present, with only a minor omission or awkward compression.
Every required fact is included accurately and naturally, with no unsupported factual claims.

metric_definitions.definition: Measures whether the generated email matches the requested tone in context.

rubric.description: The tone is absent, opposite of the requested tone, or unusable for the context.
The tone is very far from the requested tone.
The tone only loosely resembles the requested tone and is noticeably mismatched.
The tone is partly aligned but generic, uneven, or inconsistent.
The tone is mostly aligned with only small mismatches.
The tone closely and consistently matches the requested tone throughout.

metric_definitions.definition: Measures whether the output works as a professional email.

rubric.description: The output is not usable as an email.
It barely resembles a professional email or collapses greeting, body, closing, and signature into a flat single paragraph.
Some email elements are present, but structure is incomplete, hard to scan, or mostly unformatted.
Basic email content is present, but paragraphing, greeting, body, or sign-off formatting is noticeably weak.
The email is clear and professional with only minor structure or formatting rough edges.
The subject and body are polished, clear, concise, and formatted with distinct greeting, body paragraphing, and sign-off.

metrics.reason: The email includes all required facts accurately: it requests specific waiting instructions, states the meeting is on May 17 at 11:00 A.M., names the attendees Kenneth Lay, Marty Sunde, and Bob Williams, and asks where they should go or wait upon arrival. It also matches the sender identity and professional tone.

The email is concise, polite, and professional throughout. It directly asks for the specific waiting instructions needed for the May 17 meeting and uses courteous phrasing like 'Could you please clarify' and 'Thank you,' which closely matches the requested tone.

The email includes a subject line and polite greeting, but the body, closing, and signature are collapsed into one dense paragraph. It is understandable, yet the structure is incomplete and not easy to scan as a professional email.

The email includes every required fact: it schedules the project review meeting for next week, offers the three specified time slots (Tuesday 2–3 PM, Wednesday 10–11 AM, Thursday 1–2 PM), asks recipients to indicate which times work best, and says the schedule needs to be finalized soon.

The email is professional and concise, with a collaborative call for availability from the project team. It clearly states the meeting purpose, provides specific time slots, and asks recipients to confirm which options work best, matching the requested tone throughout.

The email includes the required subject, a professional greeting, a clear request with the proposed time slots, and a sign-off with sender details. However, the content is formatted as one continuous paragraph rather than having distinct body paragraphing, so the structure is present but noticeably weak.

The email includes all required facts: it references invoice 48217, states it was due on April 30, 2026, says the outstanding balance is $3,450, and asks for an update on the payment status plus the expected date for receiving the funds. It also matches the requested polite, professional follow-up tone.

The email is firm, polite, and professional throughout: it clearly follows up on the overdue invoice, states the unpaid balance, and requests an update on the expected payment date with courteous phrasing such as 'Could you please' and 'Thank you for your prompt attention.'

The email includes a subject line, greeting, body, closing, and signature, but the body is presented as one dense block of text with no paragraph breaks. The sign-off and signature are also not clearly separated, so it is somewhat hard to scan and only partially formatted.

The email includes all required facts accurately: it states the interview for the Software Engineer position at BrightPath Software Solutions was originally set for Tuesday at 2:00 PM, explains there is a conflict with the interviewer, offers Wednesday 9:00 AM and Thursday 3:00 PM as alternatives, notes the interview should last about 90 minutes, and asks the candidate to let them know if either option works.

The email closely matches the requested professional, courteous, apologetic, and concise tone. It opens with a sincere apology, explains the scheduling conflict briefly, offers the two requested alternative time slots, asks for confirmation, and closes politely without unnecessary detail.

The email includes a subject, greeting, body, and closing, but the entire message is written as one long paragraph with the sign-off and signature collapsed into the same line. It is understandable and professional, but the structure is noticeably weak and hard to scan.

All required facts are included accurately: the current date is September 5, 2025; early-bird registration closes on September 10, 2025; the conference dates are October 22 to 24, 2025; the early-bird fee is $300 and the regular fee is $400; and the email clearly encourages recipients to register soon to secure the lower rate.

The email is informative and professional, and it includes upbeat language such as "We are excited to invite you" and "take advantage of this lower rate." It clearly communicates the deadline, dates, and pricing while maintaining a consistent, polished tone throughout.

The email includes a subject, greeting, body, and closing, but they are collapsed into one flat block with no clear paragraph breaks, making it hard to scan and only barely formatted like a professional email.

The email includes all required facts accurately: it states the design review will be completed by Friday, 19 June, 2026, explains the delay as due to missing stakeholder feedback, and says the final launch date is not affected. No required fact is missing or contradicted.

The email is transparent about the delayed milestone, calmly explains the cause as missing stakeholder feedback, and reassures the team that the final launch date is unchanged. The wording is measured and supportive throughout, matching the requested tone closely and consistently.

The email has a clear subject line, a professional greeting ('Dear Team'), a coherent body that includes the milestone update and reason for the delay, and a polite sign-off ('Best regards'). The only structure rough edge is that the body is presented as a single paragraph rather than more distinct paragraphing.

The email includes all required facts accurately: it references the Q3 roadmap priorities meeting, says the attached notes summarize key decisions and action items, and asks for corrections by end of day Thursday. The sender and affiliation also match, and there are no unsupported factual claims.

The email is clear, organized, and professional throughout: it states the meeting topic, summarizes the attached notes, requests corrections by end of day Thursday, and closes with a polite thank-you and formal sign-off.

The email includes a subject, greeting, body, closing, and signature, but they are all collapsed into a single unformatted paragraph. This makes it hard to scan and fits the rubric for a barely structured professional email.

The email includes all required facts accurately: it invites volunteers to a community cleanup on Saturday from 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM, asks them to bring gloves and wear closed-toe shoes, and states that lunch and water will be provided.

The email is consistently friendly, encouraging, and appreciative, with phrases like "We truly appreciate your time and dedication," "Thank you for your continued support," and "I look forward to seeing you there!" matching the requested tone well.

The email has a clear subject, a proper greeting, a coherent body, and an appropriate sign-off with sender affiliation. The main structure issue is that the body and closing are presented as one long block rather than in distinct paragraphs, so it is professional but not fully polished.

The email includes all required facts accurately and naturally: it says visitors must check in at the front desk, requires a government-issued photo ID for entry, and states the new policy takes effect this coming Monday. The tone is formal and informative.

The email is consistently formal and informative, using professional phrasing such as 'Please be advised,' clear policy details, and a courteous closing. It accurately conveys the update without informal or off-tone language.

The email includes a subject, greeting, body, and sign-off, but the body is written as one flat paragraph with no distinct paragraph breaks or separate closing/signature formatting, which matches a barely structured professional email.

The email includes every required fact accurately: maintenance is scheduled for Sunday from 1:00 AM to 3:00 AM, the portal may be temporarily unavailable during that window, and full service is expected to resume immediately after maintenance. It is also brief, reassuring, and professional.

The email is brief, professional, and reassuring throughout. It clearly states the scheduled maintenance window, notes the portal may be temporarily unavailable, and reassures users that service is expected to resume immediately afterward.

The email includes a subject line, greeting, body, closing, and signature, and the wording is clear and professional. The main structure issue is that the body is presented as one dense paragraph rather than being broken into more distinct paragraphs, so it is polished but not fully formatted.
